title: Bronchiolitis
oneLiner: Acute viral lower respiratory tract infection in infants <12 months; leading cause of winter admissions; hypoxia and apnoea are the threats.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Apnoea:** The baby stops breathing – no chest movement, desaturation, bradycardia. *Key finding:* Any history of apnoea (even 1 episode) in a well-appearing infant with mild tachypnoea – do not reassure; admit for monitoring.
- **Severe hypoxaemia / respiratory failure:** Silent chest, grunting, head bobbing, severe subcostal recession, SpO₂ <90% despite 15 L O₂ via non-rebreather. *Key finding:* Inability to maintain saturations >88% with 40% FiO₂ – immediate senior/ICU referral.
- **Differential mimic: Bacterial tracheitis / severe croup:** Stridor (inspiratory, not expiratory), high fever, toxic appearance. *Key finding:* Stridor is not typical bronchiolitis; if present, think airway obstruction, not small airways disease.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):** Cough, coryza (runny nose, fever usually low-grade), tachypnoea, difficulty feeding, irritability. Age <12 months (peak 2-6 months). Winter season.
- **Key exam findings:** Bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and/or expiratory wheeze. Chest hyperinflation (barrel chest, liver/spleen palpable). Subcostal / intercostal recession. Nasal flaring. Signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes). Apnoea may be first sign in neonates/ <2 months.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Viral-induced wheeze (older toddlers >12 months with atopic family history – wheeze more prominent, responds to bronchodilators). *Splitter:* Age <12 months favours bronchiolitis; age >12 months + family atopy + wheeze with no crackles = viral wheeze.
- **Asthma first presentation:** >12 months, wheeze ± reversible with salbutamol, often no crackles, atopic history.
- **Pneumonia:** Focal crackles, high fever, toxic appearance, lobar consolidation on CXR.
- **Foreign body aspiration:** Sudden-onset cough/choking, asymmetrical wheeze, unilateral hyperinflation on CXR.
- **Croup:** Barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice – not bronchiolitis.
- **Congenital heart disease (e.g. VSD with failure):** Poor feeding from birth, hepatomegaly, murmur, cyanosis – CXR shows cardiomegaly.
- **Bronchiolitis obliterans:** Older child, chronic cough after severe infection – rare, but consider if recurrent.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** SpO₂ monitoring (continuous if <92%), respiratory rate, heart rate, capillary refill. Consider nasopharyngeal aspirate for viral PCR (RSV, rhinovirus) – useful for cohorting, not mandatory for diagnosis. Check feeding ability (observe a feed).
- **Bloods:** Not routine. FBC, CRP, U&E, blood culture only if febrile >39°C, toxic, or suspected sepsis / complication. Capillary gas if severe (pH <7.3, pCO₂ >8 kPa significant).
- **Imaging:** *CXR* – not routinely needed; only if: diagnostic uncertainty (e.g. suspect pneumonia, foreign body), severe disease (ICU), or clinical deterioration. Typical findings: hyperinflation, perihilar streaking, patchy atelectasis.
- **Special tests:** No other routine. Pre-operative tests (e.g. for congenital anomalies) only if needed.
- **Diagnostic criteria (NICE):** Clinical diagnosis – age 1-12 months (up to 2 years in some guidelines) with coryza, cough, tachypnoea, crackles/wheeze, ± fever. Severity scoring: mild (SpO₂ >92%, feeding normally), moderate (SpO₂ 90-92%, mild-moderate recession, reduced feeding), severe (SpO₂ <90%, severe recession, apnoea, poor feeding).
- **Never-miss test:** **SpO₂** – a normal SpO₂ does not rule out apnoea risk, but a low SpO₂ drives escalation. Also: **observe a feed** – inability to feed is a key admission criterion.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity. **Oxygen** if SpO₂ persistently <90% (target 92-94% if on O₂). Use low-flow nasal cannula (1-2 L/min) or head-box. **Suction** nasal secretions if significant obstruction (saline drops + gentle suction – do not deep-suction). **Bronchodilators** – not routinely recommended. Trial of nebulised salbutamol (2.5 mg in 2.5 mL N/S) only if strong wheeze >12 months or known asthma – stop if no response. **High flow nasal cannula (HFNC)** if moderate-severe (starting 2 L/kg/min, FiO₂ titrate) – but only under senior guidance. **IV fluids** if unable to feed (see below).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥90% (NICE: consider stopping if stable in air for 4-6h). Monitor feeding – nasogastric (NG) feeds if oral not adequate. IV fluids if NG not tolerated or severe distress (NG safer than IV in mild-mod). Chest physiotherapy – **do not routinely perform** (no evidence). Antibiotics only if confirmed secondary pneumonia (focal CXR changes, fever >39°C, CRP significantly raised). Nebulised adrenaline – only in high-dependency/ICU for severe disease (not ward).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most recover completely. No specific follow-up routine. Advise parents to avoid smoke exposure. Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants (ex-preterm <35wks, chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease) – given monthly through RSV season.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If NG feeds, start with expressed breast milk or formula, 5-10 mL/kg every 2-3h, increase as tolerated. If IV, use 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose + 0.2% KCl (see BNFc for rates); typical maintenance: 100 mL/kg/24h for first 10 kg. **VTE prophylaxis:** Not applicable (infants). **Diet:** Continue breast/formula feeds if able; if on O₂ or respiratory distress, consider NG. **Observations:** 4-hourly (respiratory rate, SpO₂, heart rate) – increase to 1-2 hourly if moderate disease.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen:** via nasal cannula 1-2 L/min or head-box. Target SpO₂ ≥90% (if on O₂).
  - **Salbutamol (trial only – not routine):** Nebulised 2.5 mg (in 2.5 mL N/S) for infants <12 months? – [check local policy] – generally not recommended by NICE.
  - **Ipratropium bromide (same caveat):** Nebulised 125 micrograms (0.5 mL) – only if severe wheeze not responding to salbutamol.
  - **Adrenaline (nebulised):** 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 (max 5 mL) – only in severe disease under senior/ICU advice.
  - **Fluid replacement:** IV 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose (or 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose if hypoglycaemic). Add 20 mmol KCl per 500 mL after ensuring renal function normal.
- **Common alternatives:** None for bronchiolitis itself.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid beta-blockers (even eye drops) in active bronchiolitis – can worsen wheeze.
  - Avoid sedatives (e.g. antihistamines, cough suppressants) – risk of respiratory depression.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not applicable in this age group (fluids adjusted for weight, not renal function).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Over-hydration with IV fluids** – risk of SIADH/respiratory distress; do not exceed 100% maintenance unless dehydrated (check U&E). Also **using nebulised bronchodilators routinely** – may cause tachycardia, hypoxia due to increased oxygen demand; only trial if strong wheeze, and stop if no clear benefit.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring HFNC or CPAP.
  - Apnoea (especially in <2 months or preterm).
  - Secondary bacterial pneumonia (focal consolidation, persistent fever).
  - Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance (from poor feeding).
  - Pneumothorax (rare, from high airway pressures).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent wheeze / asthma-like symptoms in later childhood (some evidence, but most recover fully).
  - Bronchiolitis obliterans (very rare – post-viral, chronic cough, irreversible airflow obstruction).
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality <1% in term infants. Most recover in 7-10 days. Peak severity days 3-5. Hospital stay average 3-5 days. Risk factors for severe disease: prematurity, chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease, immunodeficiency, age <3 months.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always observe a feed** before discharge – if the baby cannot take 50% of usual volume, do not send home.
- **Check for apnoea history** in every case – even a single 15-second pause in a well-looking baby mandates admission for monitoring.
- **Suction nasal secretions** before every feed and assessment – it can dramatically improve respiratory effort and feeding.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t routinely start bronchodilators** – they do not improve outcomes in true bronchiolitis and may cause harm.
- **Don’t routinely perform CXR** – radiation without benefit if classic presentation.
- **Don’t use cough suppressants or chest physiotherapy** – they increase distress and do not clear secretions.
- **Don’t send home a child who needs O₂** – only discharge when stable in air for 4-6 hours and feeding adequately.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Breathing becomes faster or harder (grunting, head bobbing, unable to complete feed or speak), blue lips or skin, pauses in breathing (apnoea), poor feeding (<50% usual volume for >2 feeds), fever >38.5°C for >24h.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2-3 days if not improving. No routine hospital follow-up.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:** **NEWS2** – not validated in infants. Use local paediatric early warning score (e.g. PEWS). Key thresholds: SpO₂ <90% despite 40% FiO₂, apnoea (any), grunting or head bobbing, severe recession, lactate >2 mmol/L, pH <7.25, pCO₂ >8 kPa, HR >180 or <80, reduced consciousness.
- **Immediate crash team call:** Apnoea with bradycardia, absent pulse, cyanosis.

rawMarkdown:
# Bronchiolitis
**One-liner:** Acute viral lower respiratory tract infection in infants <12 months; leading cause of winter admissions; hypoxia and apnoea are the threats.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Apnoea:** The baby stops breathing – no chest movement, desaturation, bradycardia. *Key finding:* Any history of apnoea (even 1 episode) in a well-appearing infant with mild tachypnoea – do not reassure; admit for monitoring.
- **Severe hypoxaemia / respiratory failure:** Silent chest, grunting, head bobbing, severe subcostal recession, SpO₂ <90% despite 15 L O₂ via non-rebreather. *Key finding:* Inability to maintain saturations >88% with 40% FiO₂ – immediate senior/ICU referral.
- **Differential mimic: Bacterial tracheitis / severe croup:** Stridor (inspiratory, not expiratory), high fever, toxic appearance. *Key finding:* Stridor is not typical bronchiolitis; if present, think airway obstruction, not small airways disease.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3-5):** Cough, coryza (runny nose, fever usually low-grade), tachypnoea, difficulty feeding, irritability. Age <12 months (peak 2-6 months). Winter season.
- **Key exam findings:** Bilateral fine inspiratory crackles and/or expiratory wheeze. Chest hyperinflation (barrel chest, liver/spleen palpable). Subcostal / intercostal recession. Nasal flaring. Signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes). Apnoea may be first sign in neonates/ <2 months.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Viral-induced wheeze (older toddlers >12 months with atopic family history – wheeze more prominent, responds to bronchodilators). *Splitter:* Age <12 months favours bronchiolitis; age >12 months + family atopy + wheeze with no crackles = viral wheeze.
- **Asthma first presentation:** >12 months, wheeze ± reversible with salbutamol, often no crackles, atopic history.
- **Pneumonia:** Focal crackles, high fever, toxic appearance, lobar consolidation on CXR.
- **Foreign body aspiration:** Sudden-onset cough/choking, asymmetrical wheeze, unilateral hyperinflation on CXR.
- **Croup:** Barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarse voice – not bronchiolitis.
- **Congenital heart disease (e.g. VSD with failure):** Poor feeding from birth, hepatomegaly, murmur, cyanosis – CXR shows cardiomegaly.
- **Bronchiolitis obliterans:** Older child, chronic cough after severe infection – rare, but consider if recurrent.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** SpO₂ monitoring (continuous if <92%), respiratory rate, heart rate, capillary refill. Consider nasopharyngeal aspirate for viral PCR (RSV, rhinovirus) – useful for cohorting, not mandatory for diagnosis. Check feeding ability (observe a feed).
- **Bloods:** Not routine. FBC, CRP, U&E, blood culture only if febrile >39°C, toxic, or suspected sepsis / complication. Capillary gas if severe (pH <7.3, pCO₂ >8 kPa significant).
- **Imaging:** *CXR* – not routinely needed; only if: diagnostic uncertainty (e.g. suspect pneumonia, foreign body), severe disease (ICU), or clinical deterioration. Typical findings: hyperinflation, perihilar streaking, patchy atelectasis.
- **Special tests:** No other routine. Pre-operative tests (e.g. for congenital anomalies) only if needed.
- **Diagnostic criteria (NICE):** Clinical diagnosis – age 1-12 months (up to 2 years in some guidelines) with coryza, cough, tachypnoea, crackles/wheeze, ± fever. Severity scoring: mild (SpO₂ >92%, feeding normally), moderate (SpO₂ 90-92%, mild-moderate recession, reduced feeding), severe (SpO₂ <90%, severe recession, apnoea, poor feeding).
- **Never-miss test:** **SpO₂** – a normal SpO₂ does not rule out apnoea risk, but a low SpO₂ drives escalation. Also: **observe a feed** – inability to feed is a key admission criterion.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess severity. **Oxygen** if SpO₂ persistently <90% (target 92-94% if on O₂). Use low-flow nasal cannula (1-2 L/min) or head-box. **Suction** nasal secretions if significant obstruction (saline drops + gentle suction – do not deep-suction). **Bronchodilators** – not routinely recommended. Trial of nebulised salbutamol (2.5 mg in 2.5 mL N/S) only if strong wheeze >12 months or known asthma – stop if no response. **High flow nasal cannula (HFNC)** if moderate-severe (starting 2 L/kg/min, FiO₂ titrate) – but only under senior guidance. **IV fluids** if unable to feed (see below).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue O₂ to target SpO₂ ≥90% (NICE: consider stopping if stable in air for 4-6h). Monitor feeding – nasogastric (NG) feeds if oral not adequate. IV fluids if NG not tolerated or severe distress (NG safer than IV in mild-mod). Chest physiotherapy – **do not routinely perform** (no evidence). Antibiotics only if confirmed secondary pneumonia (focal CXR changes, fever >39°C, CRP significantly raised). Nebulised adrenaline – only in high-dependency/ICU for severe disease (not ward).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Most recover completely. No specific follow-up routine. Advise parents to avoid smoke exposure. Palivizumab prophylaxis for high-risk infants (ex-preterm <35wks, chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease) – given monthly through RSV season.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** If NG feeds, start with expressed breast milk or formula, 5-10 mL/kg every 2-3h, increase as tolerated. If IV, use 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose + 0.2% KCl (see BNFc for rates); typical maintenance: 100 mL/kg/24h for first 10 kg. **VTE prophylaxis:** Not applicable (infants). **Diet:** Continue breast/formula feeds if able; if on O₂ or respiratory distress, consider NG. **Observations:** 4-hourly (respiratory rate, SpO₂, heart rate) – increase to 1-2 hourly if moderate disease.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Oxygen:** via nasal cannula 1-2 L/min or head-box. Target SpO₂ ≥90% (if on O₂).
  - **Salbutamol (trial only – not routine):** Nebulised 2.5 mg (in 2.5 mL N/S) for infants <12 months? – [check local policy] – generally not recommended by NICE.
  - **Ipratropium bromide (same caveat):** Nebulised 125 micrograms (0.5 mL) – only if severe wheeze not responding to salbutamol.
  - **Adrenaline (nebulised):** 0.5 mL/kg of 1:1000 (max 5 mL) – only in severe disease under senior/ICU advice.
  - **Fluid replacement:** IV 0.45% NaCl + 5% glucose (or 0.9% NaCl + 5% glucose if hypoglycaemic). Add 20 mmol KCl per 500 mL after ensuring renal function normal.
- **Common alternatives:** None for bronchiolitis itself.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Avoid beta-blockers (even eye drops) in active bronchiolitis – can worsen wheeze.
  - Avoid sedatives (e.g. antihistamines, cough suppressants) – risk of respiratory depression.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Not applicable in this age group (fluids adjusted for weight, not renal function).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Over-hydration with IV fluids** – risk of SIADH/respiratory distress; do not exceed 100% maintenance unless dehydrated (check U&E). Also **using nebulised bronchodilators routinely** – may cause tachycardia, hypoxia due to increased oxygen demand; only trial if strong wheeze, and stop if no clear benefit.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring HFNC or CPAP.
  - Apnoea (especially in <2 months or preterm).
  - Secondary bacterial pneumonia (focal consolidation, persistent fever).
  - Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance (from poor feeding).
  - Pneumothorax (rare, from high airway pressures).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent wheeze / asthma-like symptoms in later childhood (some evidence, but most recover fully).
  - Bronchiolitis obliterans (very rare – post-viral, chronic cough, irreversible airflow obstruction).

### Prognosis
- Mortality <1% in term infants. Most recover in 7-10 days. Peak severity days 3-5. Hospital stay average 3-5 days. Risk factors for severe disease: prematurity, chronic lung disease, congenital heart disease, immunodeficiency, age <3 months.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always observe a feed** before discharge – if the baby cannot take 50% of usual volume, do not send home.
- **Check for apnoea history** in every case – even a single 15-second pause in a well-looking baby mandates admission for monitoring.
- **Suction nasal secretions** before every feed and assessment – it can dramatically improve respiratory effort and feeding.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t routinely start bronchodilators** – they do not improve outcomes in true bronchiolitis and may cause harm.
- **Don’t routinely perform CXR** – radiation without benefit if classic presentation.
- **Don’t use cough suppressants or chest physiotherapy** – they increase distress and do not clear secretions.
- **Don’t send home a child who needs O₂** – only discharge when stable in air for 4-6 hours and feeding adequately.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Breathing becomes faster or harder (grunting, head bobbing, unable to complete feed or speak), blue lips or skin, pauses in breathing (apnoea), poor feeding (<50% usual volume for >2 feeds), fever >38.5°C for >24h.
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 2-3 days if not improving. No routine hospital follow-up.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:** **NEWS2** – not validated in infants. Use local paediatric early warning score (e.g. PEWS). Key thresholds: SpO₂ <90% despite 40% FiO₂, apnoea (any), grunting or head bobbing, severe recession, lactate >2 mmol/L, pH <7.25, pCO₂ >8 kPa, HR >180 or <80, reduced consciousness.
- **Immediate crash team call:** Apnoea with bradycardia, absent pulse, cyanosis.
